feed.title: Stories by Anshit Vishwakarma on Medium
feed.description: Stories by Anshit Vishwakarma on Medium

items.title: Fashion’s Secret Weapon: Unleashing Data Science in the Apparel Retail Industry
items.author: Anshit Vishwakarma
items.description:
Data Science has permeated nearly every facet of our daily lives. From checking the morning news to scrolling through Instagram reels, planning weekend activities based on weather forecasts (a familiar plight for those in Bangalore), or even utilizing GPT models to streamline your code, the influence of data science is inescapable. It brings to mind a famous line from the movie “3 Idiots”: “From the tip of the pen to the bottom of your pants’ zip, it’s all Data Science, sir.”

Yet, amidst our immersion in data-driven algorithms created by direct-to-consumer (D2C) companies, we often fail to fully appreciate the extent to which Data Science has infiltrated the world of business-to-business (B2B) operations, particularly within the retail industry — an industry that this article will delve into.

The rise of e-commerce behemoths like Amazon and Flipkart has revolutionized consumer behavior. Shoppers can now peruse product catalogs at any hour, as opposed to adhering to traditional store hours of 10 AM to 9 PM. Beyond this convenience, they can compare products across various sellers, considering factors like price, quality, and delivery time. The option to return a product within 15 days, whether satisfied or not, has left conventional retailers struggling to compete with these giants. Consequently, retailers such as Walmart, H&M, and Zara have been compelled to pivot toward a more omnichannel approach to conducting business. The magnitude of this shift in consumer behavior cannot be underestimated; we’ve witnessed companies that failed to adapt to these changes shuttering their doors (a recent example being Big Bazaar in India). But does this imply that retail companies are left with no competitive edge against e-commerce giants?

In my personal experience, there are still areas where traditional retail holds an advantage over e-commerce. Take apparel, for instance: people tend to purchase clothing items such as T-shirts, socks, shoes, and undergarments from e-commerce websites, where fitting is less of a concern, as they typically have a size in mind that suits them. However, when it comes to items like chinos, jeans, suits, and the like, individuals often prefer to try them on in stores, ensuring the fit complements their body shape before making a decision. The tactile experience of feeling the fabric also adds value to the in-store shopping process. This can be likened to the shift from traditional pen-and-paper exams to online testing methods for exams like JEE, NEET, and BITSAT. While many have embraced the convenience of online exams, some, like me, still prefer the tangible nature of pen and paper, allowing us to view multiple pages of questions simultaneously, jot down calculations alongside problems, and revisit skipped questions at our leisure.

Most retail companies are now focusing on enhancing the in-store shopping experience, a commendable direction to take. While e-commerce apps are accessible round-the-clock, a visit to the shopping mall may occur just once a week, akin to the difference between streaming a movie on an OTT platform and watching it in a theater. However, what truly constitutes a remarkable shopping experience, and how are retail companies utilizing data science to address the underlying challenges? We will explore each of these factors in depth in the following sections.

Assortment Planning

Step into any retail store, and what do you see? The entire space is meticulously organized, guiding you effortlessly through a carefully curated array of products. It’s like a symphony of choices choreographed for your convenience. This choreography is what we call ‘Assortment Planning.’ In the retail industry, it’s a secret sauce, an art form, and a science that defines a store’s success by harmonizing product offerings with customer preferences, market dynamics, and strategic objectives.

Assortment planning is akin to retail wizardry that turns a store into a captivating shopping journey. Picture this: You wander into the “Men’s Jeans” section, and there they are — an ensemble of brands, proudly displayed on mannequins, neatly hung on racks, and sorted by size. It’s a shopping ballet designed to cater to your desires, and it’s all powered by data.

Data science, the magician behind the curtain, wields historical sales data, dissects market trends, and deciphers countless other relevant factors to forecast demand for various products. Using intricate machine learning models like time series forecasting and demand prediction algorithms, data science unveils insights into what products are likely to be the stars of the future. This foresight empowers retailers to stock their shelves with precisely what they’ll be craving.

But data science doesn’t stop there. It dives deeper into the nuances of your shopping behavior. It’s the Sherlock Holmes of retail, uncovering the mysteries of what products tend to be snatched up together. Think about it — you grab a pair of shoes, and voilà, the socks right next to them start calling your name. That’s not luck; it’s the brilliance of data-driven recommendations. It’s the retailer’s way of saying, “Why not take both and save a little?” This is how data science orchestrates the art of cross-selling.

Now, let’s step backstage into the world of apparel retail chains like Shoppers Stop, Aditya Birla Fashion, etc. Here, buyers play a fascinating, yet challenging, game of prediction. They have to place orders with vendors a whopping six months before a season kicks off. Imagine ordering swimwear in the heart of winter! The question that haunts them is this: How can a business predict the future of fashion based on sales data that only stretches to June 2022 for a season starting in January 2023?

This is where the real magic happens. The trick is to keep an eye on the global fashion stage — think Paris Fashion Week — and spot emerging trends in the making. But here’s the ultimate hack: whatever haute couture the top-tier brands like Balenciaga, Louis Vuitton, or Gucci are flaunting today, you’ll find it replicated in the market in just a few months, in a more budget-friendly version. Retail juggernauts like H&M and Zara have transformed their supply chains into nimble machines, churning out affordable fashion replicas in the blink of an eye.

In the world of assortment planning, data science is the compass, the crystal ball, and the magician’s wand rolled into one. It turns raw data into retail poetry, crafting the perfect retail symphony where you’re not just a shopper; you’re the star of the show. So next time you stroll through your favorite store, remember that the products on those shelves aren’t there by chance — they’re there by data-driven design.

Markdown Planning

In today’s retail landscape, shoppers have become savvy deal hunters. It’s almost as if they have a sixth sense for discounts. When it comes to apparel, initial profit margins can be enticing, but here’s the catch: to make way for the latest trends and keep up with the ever-evolving competition, retailers need to master the art of markdowns.

Welcome to the world of “Markdown Planning” in retail — a strategic dance of pricing and inventory management. It’s the secret sauce that transforms underperforming products into hot commodities, all while minimizing losses and creating space for fresh inventory. The primary mission here is to decode how our products will perform under different price adjustments and recommend price points that maximize profitability. Picture this: a retail stage set for grand sales events that coincide with major celebrations like Eid, New Year, and Diwali. To achieve this harmonious blend of sales and festivities, retailers must predict how products will fare at various potential discount percentages.

Sounds straightforward, right? Well, here’s the plot twist: Retailers introduce new fashion items each year, and these newcomers lack a sales history. So, how do you predict the audience’s applause for these newcomers?

Enter the data scientists, the magicians behind the scenes. They employ advanced techniques like clustering to group products with similar selling patterns and attributes during the season. It’s like arranging products into squads, each with its unique character and style. Once these squads are formed, data scientists perform a sophisticated dance of regression analysis. This enables them to predict how each product will perform at various markdown percentages. Imagine it as predicting the plot twists and turns of a gripping story. Armed with these insights, retailers can strategically set price points that maximize profit margins while staying within their budget.

Markdown planning isn’t just about discounts; it’s about crafting a captivating retail experience. It’s about giving customers what they desire — the thrill of a great deal. It’s also about keeping the retail stage dynamic, ready for the next show-stopping collection.

So, the next time you snag that irresistible markdown, remember that it’s not just a number — it’s the result of meticulous data analysis and strategic planning, designed to ensure both retailers and customers leave the store with smiles on their faces. It’s the magic behind the sale, making retail a thrilling experience for all involved.

Too much to chew on for a first article, isn’t it? But fear not, we’re just getting started on our journey through the retail world’s data-driven wonders. Assortment and Markdown planning are just the tip of the retail iceberg, and there’s a treasure trove of challenges waiting to be explored.

In my upcoming articles, I’ll be your guide through the twists and turns of supply chain conundrums and more. Together, we’ll uncover how data science dons its superhero cape to rescue businesses from the brink.

So, until next time when we dive even deeper into the exciting world of data science in retail, it’s time to bid adieu. Keep those curiosity engines revved up and stay tuned for more data-driven adventures. Thanks for reading. Goodbye for now! 🚀🛒
